questions.question: Define a distributed system and explain its key characteristics.
keyPoints: Collection of independent computers
Appears as single coherent system to users
Network-based communication
Key characteristics: transparency, scalability, resource sharing, reliability

questions.question: Explain the different types of transparency in distributed systems with examples.
keyPoints: Access transparency - hides differences in data representation
Location transparency - hides where resources are physically located
Migration transparency - allows movement of resources without user knowledge
Failure transparency - hides failures and recovery
Replication transparency - hides existence of multiple copies

questions.question: Differentiate between client-server and peer-to-peer architectures.
keyPoints: Client-server: central server provides services, clients request services
Peer-to-peer: all nodes equal, can act as both client and server
Client-server has single point of failure concern
P2P offers better scalability and resilience
Examples: Web (client-server), BitTorrent (P2P)

questions.question: What is the CAP theorem? Explain its significance in distributed system design.
keyPoints: CAP stands for Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
States only two of three can be guaranteed simultaneously
Partitions are inevitable in distributed systems
Designers must choose between consistency and availability during partitions
Impacts database and storage system design decisions

questions.question: Explain the concept of mutual exclusion in distributed systems. Mention any one algorithm.
keyPoints: Ensures only one process accesses critical section at a time
Challenges: no shared memory, network delays, failures
Algorithms: Centralized, Distributed, Token-ring
Centralized uses coordinator to grant permission
Distributed uses voting or permission from majority

questions.question: What is middleware in distributed systems? Explain its functions.
keyPoints: Software layer between applications and operating system
Provides common services hiding heterogeneity
Functions: communication, transaction management, security
Examples: CORBA, Java RMI, SOAP
Enables interoperability between different applications

questions.question: Explain the concept of clock synchronization in distributed systems. Why is it important?
keyPoints: Synchronizes clocks across different nodes
Important for ordering events, concurrency control
Algorithms: NTP, Cristian's algorithm, Berkeley algorithm
NTP uses hierarchical master-slave approach
Without synchronization, event ordering becomes ambiguous

questions.question: Explain the client-server model in detail. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and compare it with the peer-to-peer model.
keyPoints: Client-server: clients request services, servers provide services
Advantages: centralized resources, easier security management, better control
Disadvantages: single point of failure, scalability limits, network dependency
P2P: all nodes equal, decentralized, no single point of failure
P2P advantages: scalability, resilience, direct sharing
P2P disadvantages: harder security, less control, discovery challenges
Modern systems often combine both approaches

questions.question: What are the challenges in achieving mutual exclusion in distributed systems? Explain any two distributed mutual exclusion algorithms with their merits and demerits.
keyPoints: Challenges: no shared memory, message delays, process failures, network partitions
Centralized algorithm: coordinator grants permission, simple but single point of failure
Distributed algorithm: requires majority votes, more fault-tolerant but complex
Token-ring algorithm: token circulates, no starvation but overhead of token management
Comparison: centralized simpler but not fault-tolerant, distributed more robust but message complexity high

questions.question: Discuss the issues related to deadlock handling in distributed systems. Compare deadlock prevention, avoidance, and detection approaches.
keyPoints: Distributed deadlock involves processes across multiple machines
Prevention: restrict resource request order, preempt resources, eliminates wait-for cycles
Avoidance: Banker's algorithm adapted for distributed, requires resource state information
Detection: build and analyze wait-for graph across machines, more commonly used
Challenges: communication overhead, false deadlocks, partial failures
Trade-offs: prevention avoids deadlocks but restricts concurrency, detection allows more concurrency but has overhead

questions.question: Explain the concept of replication in distributed systems. Discuss the different consistency models and their trade-offs.
keyPoints: Replication: storing multiple copies of data on different nodes
Purposes: reliability, performance, availability
Consistency models: Strong (all reads see most recent write), Eventual (updates propagate eventually), Weak (may see stale data)
Trade-offs: stronger consistency means higher latency, weaker improves performance
CAP theorem implications: choose consistency or availability during partitions
Primary-backup vs multi-master replication approaches
Quorum-based replication for balancing consistency and availability
